metadata.title: Short-term fasting abolishes the sex-related difference in GH and leptin secretion in humans
metadata.demographics: Fifteen normal adult volunteers: eight men (age 29.3 ± 1.2 yr, BMI 22.2 ± 0.5 kg/m²) and seven women (age 28.0 ± 0.8 yr, BMI 20.1 ± 0.7 kg/m²). All female subjects had regular menses and were studied in their early follicular phase.

evidence.quote: Short-term fasting abolishes the sex-related difference in GH and leptin secretion in humans

We studied growth hormone (GH) and leptin secretion in eight male (age 29.3 6 1.2 yr, body mass index 22.2 6 0.5 kg/m2) and seven female normal subjects (28.0 6 0.8 yr, 20.1 6 0.7 kg/m2) before and after 36 h of fasting
In the fed state, 8-h mean GH and leptin concentrations were higher in females (P < 0.05 and P < 0.0001, respectively)
All female subjects had regular menses and were studied in their early follicular phase
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 279: E411–E416, 2000
eight male (age 29.3 6 1.2 yr, body mass index 22.2 6 0.5 kg/m2)
before and after 36 h of fasting
all subjects had a 200-kcal breakfast (60% carbohydrate) at 8 AM
From 8 AM to 4 PM, blood samples were taken every 30 min from an antecubital vein
Serum leptin levels (μg/l) were measured in duplicate at each time point by RIA using commercial kits (Human Leptin RIA, Linco Research, St. Charles, MO).

methods_participants.label: Female inclusion criterion - menstrual status
methods_participants.value: regular menses, studied in early follicular phase

evidence.quote: All female subjects had regular menses and were studied in their early follicular phase.
were on a classical Mediterranean diet (≈50% carbohydrates, 30% lipids, and 20% proteins)
The limit of sensitivity was 0.5 mg/l

methods_participants.label: Statistical Analysis Method
methods_participants.value: Two-way ANOVA with gender and fasting factors

evidence.quote: The statistical analysis of the data was performed by a two-way ANOVA: between-subject (gender) and within-subject (fasting) factors and their interaction were considered

narrative_results.statement: Mean leptin concentration in men was lower than in women before fasting

evidence.quote: Mean leptin concentration in men (2.7 ± 0.5 mg/l) was lower (P<0.0001) than in women (5.9±0.5 mg/l; Figs. 2-3).

narrative_results.statement: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased independently of gender

evidence.quote: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased (P < 0.001) independently of gender, whereas IGFBP-3, ALS, and GHBP were unchanged (Fig. 1).

narrative_results.statement: Gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels are no longer detectable after fasting

evidence.quote: Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 states regular menses as inclusion criterion; Pass 2 specifies early follicular phase, which is incompatible with regular menses requirement

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: all subjects had a 200-kcal breakfast (60% carbohydrate) at 8 AM

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: From 8 AM to 4 PM, blood samples were taken every 30 min from an antecubital vein

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Serum GH levels (mg/l) were measured in duplicate at each timepoint by both immunoradiometric assay [IRMA; human GH, CTK, Sorin, Saluggia, Italy]
and immunofunctional assay (IFA; in-house method, Neuroendocrine Unit laboratory, Munich, Germany)

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Serum GH levels (mg/l) were measured in duplicate at each time point by both immunoradiometric assay [IRMA; human GH, CTK, Sorin, Saluggia, Italy]

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Serum insulin levels (mU/ml) were measured in duplicate by the RIA method (INSIK-5, Sorin)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: immunofunctional assay (IFA; in-house method, Neuroendocrine Unit laboratory, Munich, Germany)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Serum IGF-I levels (mg/l) were measured in duplicate by the RIA method (Nichols Institute of Diagnostics, San Juan Capistrano, CA) after acid-ethanol extraction to avoid interference by binding proteins
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The sensitivity of the IRMA method was 0.15 mg/l

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: All female subjects had regular menses and were studied in their early follicular phase
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Serum leptin levels (mg/l) were measured in duplicate at each time point by RIA using commercial kits (Human Leptin RIA, Linco Research, St. Charles, MO)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: All subjects had had stable weight in the last 6 mo before entering the study protocol
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The limit of sensitivity was 0.5 mg/l

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: were on a classical Mediterranean diet (≈50% carbohydrates, 30% lipids, and 20% proteins)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Serum insulin levels (mU/ml) were measured in duplicate by the RIA method (INSIK-5, Sorin)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: From 8 AM to 4 PM, blood samples were taken every 30 min from an antecubital vein of the forearm
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Serum IGF-I levels (mg/l) were measured in duplicate by the RIA method (Nichols Institute of Diagnostics, San Juan Capistrano, CA) after acid-ethanol extraction to avoid interference by binding proteins

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Mean leptin concentration in men was lower than in women
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Mean leptin concentration in men was lower than in women before fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: When assayed by IRMA, mean GH concentration in men was lower than in women
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: When assayed by IRMA, mean GH concentration in men was lower than in women before fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes. There was significant interaction between gender and fasting (P < 0.05 for GH and P < 0.005 for leptin).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased (P < 0.001) independently of gender, whereas IGFBP-3, ALS, and GHBP were unchanged (Fig. 1).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased independently of gender

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men before any decrease in total IGF-I, as well as in IGFBP-3 and ALS levels.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes. There was significant interaction between gender and fasting (P < 0.05 for GH and P < 0.005 for leptin).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Fasting increased GH and decreased leptin in both sexes with significant interaction between gender and fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased (P < 0.001) independently of gender, whereas IGFBP-3, ALS, and GHBP were unchanged (Fig. 1).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Females showed a lower GH increase (P < 0.05 in terms of percent increase above baseline) than males independently of the assay used (males vs. females: IRMA, 6.2 ± 1.1 vs. 5.4 ± 1.2 mg/l; IFA, 3.3 ± 0.7 vs. 2.7 ± 0.5 mg/l).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased independently of gender
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Females showed a lower GH increase than males after fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: On the other hand, females showed a higher leptin decrease (P < 0.05 in terms of percent decrease below baseline) than males (males vs. females: 0.3 ± 0.2 vs. 3.2 ± 0.5 mg/l, P < 0.001).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels are no longer detectable after fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Females showed a higher leptin decrease than males after fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Females showed a lower GH increase (P < 0.05 in terms of percent increase above baseline) than males independently of the assay used (males vs. females: IRMA, 6.2 ± 1.1 vs. 5.4 ± 1.2 mg/l; IFA, 3.3 ± 0.7 vs. 2.7 ± 0.5 mg/l).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Females showed a lower GH increase than males after fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: On the other hand, females showed a higher leptin decrease (P < 0.05 in terms of percent decrease below baseline) than males (males vs. females: 0.3 ± 0.2 vs. 3.2 ± 0.5 mg/l, P < 0.001).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In fact, short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men before any decrease in total IGF-I, as well as in IGFBP-3 and ALS levels.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Females showed a higher leptin decrease than males after fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men

semantic_groups.rationale: Both describe 7 female participants; minor wording differences ('Participant N - Females' vs 'Study Population - Females') are negligible
Identical information: 36 hours fasting duration with identical formatting
Both report 200 kcal breakfast; Pass 2 adds macronutrient composition (60% carbohydrate) not in Pass 1, but core caloric value is identical
Both report 800 kcal lunch; Pass 2 provides detailed macronutrient breakdown (50% carbohydrate, 30% lipid, 20% protein) absent in Pass 1, but core value matches
Both describe blood sampling every 30 minutes; only capitalization and formatting differ
Both identify GH assay method 1 as IRMA (immunoradiometric assay); formatting and order of abbreviation/full name differ minimally
Both identify GH assay method 2 as IFA (immunofunctional assay); only formatting and presentation order differ
Both identify leptin assay as RIA (radioimmunoassay); only formatting and abbreviation placement differ
Both describe insulin assay using RIA/Radioimmunoassay method with identical meaning
Both describe IGF-I assay using RIA with acid-ethanol extraction, same methodology
Pass 1 states regular menses as inclusion criterion; Pass 2 specifies early follicular phase, which is incompatible with regular menses requirement
Both state weight stability requirement of 6 months, minor wording variation
Both describe Mediterranean diet inclusion criterion; Pass 2 adds nutritional composition details not contradicting Pass 1
Pass 1 contains sampling timeframe information; Pass 2 is empty
Both state 8 male participants, identical fact with minor wording difference
Pass 2 contains sampling protocol duration information; Pass 1 is empty
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains GH IRMA sensitivity data (0.15 mg/l)
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains Leptin assay sensitivity data (0.5 mg/l via RIA)
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains IGF-I assay sensitivity data (0.1 mg/l via RIA)
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains Estradiol assay method (RIA)
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains Testosterone assay method (RIA)
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains statistical analysis method (two-way ANOVA)
Pass 2 adds 'before fasting' qualifier which provides temporal context; same core fact about gender difference in GH concentration
Pass 2 adds detail about gender-fasting interaction; both describe fasting effects on GH and leptin, with Pass 2 providing additional nuance
Identical statements describing gender differences in fasting response for GH and leptin
Identical statements about fasting effects on IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA independent of gender
Pass 2 adds 'in humans' qualifier; same core fact about fasting eliminating gender-related differences in GH and leptin
Identical statements about gender difference in GH response to fasting
Identical statements about gender difference in leptin response to fasting
Pass 2 adds 'before fasting' temporal qualifier; same core fact about gender difference in leptin concentration
